content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 17, 2016

Craig A. Steele
City Attorney
City of Seal Beach
355 South Grand Avenue, 40th Floor
Los Angeles, CA 90071-3101

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-16-106

Dear Mr. Steele:

     This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Seal Beach City Councilmember
Mike Varipapa regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and
not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest,
Section 1090, or incompatible offices under Section 1099. We also are not a finder of fact when
rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your
facts are complete and accurate.

QUESTION

     Does the Act prohibit the Councilmember from simultaneously serving as a member of the
Orange County Transportation Authority's ("OCTA") Board of Directors while continuing his
present employment as a Senior Transportation Engineer for the California Department of
Transportation ("Caltrans")?

CONCLUSION

     No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from simultaneously serving in both
government offices because the Councilmember would not have a financial interest that could give
rise to a conflict of interest under the Act.

FACTS

     You are the City Attorney for the City of Seal Beach and the authorized representative of
Seal Beach City Councilmember Mike Varipapa.

_________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-106
Page No. 2

The Councilmember works for Caltrans as a Senior Transportation Engineer for District 12,
an area encompassing all of Orange County. In the course of his work for Caltrans District 12, the
Councilmember serves in a supervisory and oversight role, and is responsible for project
management, contract management, planning, cost forecasting, and budget oversight on Caltrans
projects.

Caltrans District 12 has the legal authority to carry out all responsibilities relating to state
highways in District 12. Caltrans performs many functions relating to highways in District 12
through its own forces and with its own funds. In other cases, Caltrans contracts with public or
private entities to fund and construct projects. Typically, Caltrans contracts with public entities for
services through "Co-Op Agreements" that set forth the obligations of each party in relation to
projects involving state highways.

The City of Seal Beach is a member of the Orange County Transportation Authority
("OCTA"), a joint powers authority formed to develop and deliver transportation solutions
throughout Orange County. OCTA is governed by a 17-member Board of Directors made up of
elected officials from its member agencies. OCTA Board members are selected by annual Board
elections and officials are separately sworn as members of the OCTA Board for defined terms of
office. The Caltrans District 12 Director also serves as a non-voting, ex officio member of the
OCTA Board.

OCTA receives funding from a variety of sources, the most significant of which is revenue
from a countywide sales tax imposed for transportation purposes. OCTA occasionally enters into
Co-Op Agreements with Caltrans to provide some or all of the funding and services for Caltrans
improvement projects in Orange County. In other, less frequent instances, Caltrans provides
funding to OCTA by contract for OCTA spend on programs and services.

The City of Seal Beach's current representative on the OCTA Board is leaving office in
December due to local term limits, and the Councilmember may seek to be elected the City's new
representative to the OCTA Board. On June 14, 2016, you advised that OCTA pays its Board
members a $100 per diem for each meeting of the Board, with a maximum of $500 in per diem for
each member per month.

ANALYSIS
The Act

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or in any
way using his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. A conflict of interest arises when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on an official's financial interest is material. Section 87103 sets forth the
financial interests that can give rise to a conflict of interest under the Act:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)) or in which the official is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87103(d)).

File No. A-16-106
Page No. 3

• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest
worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)

• An interest in a source of income to the official aggregating $500 or more in
value to the official within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

• An interest in a source of gifts to the official if the gifts aggregate to $460 or
more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e).)

• An interest in the official's personal finances or those of his or her immediate
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18702.5.)

The facts presented do not involve any real property interests or gifts. Therefore, we now consider
whether the Councilmember has an interest in OCTA as a business entity, as a source of income,
and whether the "personal financial effects" rule applies.

Business Entity:

A joint powers authority is a "local government agency" under the Act. (Section 82041;
Wagner Advice Letter, No. A-16-045; Muñoz Advice Letter, No. A-15-219; MaGill Advice Letter,
No. A-12-084.) OCTA is a joint powers authority. Therefore, OCTA is a local government agency.

Section 82005 defines a "business entity" as "any organization or enterprise operated for
profit, including but not limited to a sole proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint
venture, syndicate, corporation, or association." Because OCTA is not an organization or enterprise
operated for profit, it is not a "business entity" under the Act. Therefore, the Councilmember would
not have an interest in OCTA as a business entity if he serves as a member of the OCTA Board.

Source of Income:

Section 82030 defines "income" for purposes of the Act. Section 82030(b)(2) sets forth the
"governmental salary exception" to the definition of "income," and provides that "income" does not
include "[s]alary, reimbursement for expenses or per diem, and social security, disability, or other
similar benefit payments received from a state, local, or federal agency." (Section 82030(b)(2).)
Because OCTA is a "local government agency" under the Act, its payment of salary,
reimbursement for expenses or per diem, or certain benefits to the Councilmember for serving as a
member of the OCTA Board would not constitute "income" under the Act. Therefore, the
Councilmember would not have an interest in OCTA as a source of income.

Personal Finances:

A governmental decision has a material financial effect on an official's personal finances if
the official or a member of the official's immediate family will receive a measurable financial
benefit or loss because of the decision. (Regulation 18702.5(a).) OCTA pays its Board members a
$100 per diem for each meeting of the Board, with a maximum of $500 in per diem per month.
Although OCTA would compensate the Councilmember in exchange for his work on behalf of the

File No. A-16-106
Page No. 4

OCTA Board, the compensation would not have an independent, material financial effect on you or
an immediate family member's personal finances.² Therefore, the "personal financial effects" rule
would not apply.

Thus, the Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from simultaneously serving as a
member of the OCTA Board of Directors while continuing his present employment for Caltrans
District 12 because, based on the facts presented, the Councilmember would not have a financial
interest that could give rise to a conflict of interest under the Act.³

Laws Outside of the Act

Laws outside the jurisdiction of the Commission may restrict a public official from holding
two public offices simultaneously if those offices are determined to be "incompatible offices."
Moreover, agencies are required to have a "statement of incompatible activities" that also may be
implicated. As stated above, the Commission's advice is limited solely to matters arising under the
Act. In light of the facts presented, we encourage the Councilmember to review the doctrine of
incompatible offices in Section 1099 and Caltrans, the City of Seal Beach, and OCTA's statements
of incompatible activities to ensure compliance with those provisions.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl


²Notwithstanding Regulations 18702.1 through 18702.5, the financial effect of a governmental decision is not
material if it is nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant. (Regulation 18702(b).)

³ You also ask whether the Act would prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in decisions involving
OCTA in his position as Senior Transportation Engineer for Caltrans District 12. If he serves as a member of the OCTA
Board. As noted above, the Councilmember would not have a financial interest in OCTA as a business entity or a
source of income, and the "personal financial effects" rule would not apply. Therefore, the Act generally would not
prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in decisions involving OCTA because he would not have an interest that
could give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act. However, if a particular decision involving OCTA
could have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on or more of the Councilmember's interests, or if
there is a particular, identifiable contract involving OCTA that raises issues under Section 1090, we encourage you to
seek further advice providing all material facts relevant to that decision or contract.

Does the Act prohibit the Councilmember from simultaneously serving as a member of the

Transportation ("Caltrans")?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from simultaneously serving in both 
government offices because the Councilmember would not have a financial interest that could give 
rise to a conflict of interest under the Act.

management (Section 87103(d)).

worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)

value to the official within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

Although OCT A would compensate the Councilmember in exchange for his work on behalf of the

Thus, the Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from simultaneously serving as a

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit the Councilmember from simultaneously serving as a member of the 
Orange County Transportation Authority's ("OCTA") Board of Directors while continuing his 
present employment as a Senior Transportation Engineer for the California Department of 
Transportation ("Caltrans")?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from simultaneously serving in both 
government offices because the Councilmember would not have a financial interest that could give 
rise to a conflict of interest under the Act.
